message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T100 (ledgerRefs goals:G6)
Branch: implement/T100    Base commit: 55ff983 (current main HEAD)
headline: Bring the wanbond0 link UP after TUN creation (I1)
description: In device.Up (internal/device/device.go), after the TUN is created, set IFF_UP on the interface (SIOCSIFFLAGS ioctl via golang.org/x/sys — no new dependency; the repo already targets Linux, see internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go). Addressing stays operator-owned (do NOT assign addresses). Teardown behavior unchanged. Log an INFO 'interface up' with the interface name. Removes the silent-dead-tunnel footgun where writes to a DOWN tun yield EIO (relates D39/NM flush; not a duplicate of the D39 fix).
acceptance: New netns e2e test (go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e) asserts wanbond0 reports UP immediately after device.Up on both roles with NO external `ip link set up`, and that the daemon assigns no address. go test ./... green; no regression in existing device/reload tests.

IMPORTANT: the `-tags e2e` netns suite requires privileged/real-network hardware not available in your worktree — you CANNOT run it here. Write the e2e test so it COMPILES and is well-formed (`go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e` and `go build -tags e2e ./test/e2e` must pass), but validate the production change via the normal gate + any non-privileged device unit test you can add. Note in your summary that e2e hardware validation is deferred (mirrors the G2 pattern).

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. Look at internal/bind/pathsock_linux.go for the existing golang.org/x/sys/unix ioctl idiom.

If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t100-fix implement/T100` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit there, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	//go:build e2e
     2	
     3	package e2e
     4	
     5	import (
     6		"fmt"
     7		"os"
     8		"os/exec"
     9		"strconv"
    10		"strings"
    11		"testing"
    12		"time"
    13	)
    14	
    15	// pathSpec describes one emulated WAN uplink between the edge and concentrator
    16	// namespaces: a veth pair carrying a /24, with netem delay+jitter on the edge
    17	// egress. The bandwidth cap (rateMbit) and controlled loss (lossPct) are
    18	// OPTIONAL config-time impairments: both default to zero, so the DefaultPaths
    19	// topology stays uncapped and lossless and every existing P0/P1 e2e test runs
    20	// unchanged. A non-zero rateMbit makes the LINK — not the single-core userspace
    21	// WG crypto — the bottleneck ONLY when rateMbit sits below the EXECUTING host's
    22	// measured in-fixture tunnel ceiling. That ceiling is CPU/PPS-bound (both daemons
    23	// plus the load generator share the host's cores), a lower bound that scales with
    24	// core count, NOT a link-throughput spec: ~12–46 Mbit/s single-flow on a 1-vCPU
    25	// aarch64 host (docs/p0-findings.md:216-225), ~13 Mbit/s single-path (up to
    26	// ~47–87 Mbit/s FEC single-flow) on a 4-vCPU amd64 host. Sizing rule: cap < ceiling
    27	// for single-path, 2×cap < ceiling for aggregation. Below the ceiling
    28	// a standing queue can form for bufferbloat/pacing (T21/T23) work; a non-zero
    29	// lossPct injects uniform egress loss at Setup time for FEC-recovery (T25/T29)
    30	// work.
    31	type pathSpec struct {
    32		name     string
    33		edgeIP   string
    34		concIP   string
    35		edgeVeth string // <=15 chars
    36		concVeth string
    37		delayMs  int
    38		jitterMs int
    39		rateMbit int     // optional per-path bandwidth cap (netem rate); 0 = uncapped
    40		lossPct  float64 // optional config-time uniform egress loss (netem loss); 0 = lossless
    41	
    42		// deferEdgeAddr, when true, makes Setup create and bring up the edge veth WITHOUT
    43		// assigning edgeIP to it: the interface exists (so tc/netem still applies and the
    44		// link is up) but the configured source_addr is not yet owned by any interface —
    45		// the well-formed-but-not-yet-assignable condition T51's tolerant Open() defers and
    46		// T55's background reconcile later promotes (T60). AddEdgeAddr adds the withheld
    47		// address later. Zero value (false) is the default for every existing path, so
    48		// Setup's behaviour for DefaultPaths and every other caller is unchanged.
    49		deferEdgeAddr bool
    50	}
    51	
    52	// DefaultPaths are the two emulated links: Starlink-like (low latency, jittery)
    53	// and 5G-like (higher latency, stable).
    54	var DefaultPaths = []pathSpec{
    55		{name: "starlink", edgeIP: "10.100.1.1", concIP: "10.100.1.2", edgeVeth: "wbAe", concVeth: "wbAc", delayMs: 45, jitterMs: 10},
    56		{name: "cellular", edgeIP: "10.100.2.1", concIP: "10.100.2.2", edgeVeth: "wbBe", concVeth: "wbBc", delayMs: 64, jitterMs: 0},
    57	}
    58	
    59	// Topology is a two-namespace netns/netem fixture. The edge side is the current
    60	// (re-exec'd) network namespace; the concentrator side is a child process's
    61	// network namespace, addressed by PID so no writable /run/netns is required.
    62	type Topology struct {
    63		t      *testing.T
    64		holder *exec.Cmd
    65		pid    int
    66		paths  []pathSpec
    67	}
    68	
    69	// Setup builds the two-path topology from DefaultPaths (uncapped, lossless). It
    70	// requires CAP_NET_ADMIN, which the e2e TestMain provides via an unprivileged
    71	// user+net namespace (`unshare -Urmn`).
    72	func Setup(t *testing.T) *Topology {
    73		return SetupWithPaths(t, DefaultPaths)
    74	}
    75	
    76	// SetupWithPaths builds the topology from an explicit path set, allowing a test
    77	// to opt into the optional per-path bandwidth cap (rateMbit) and/or config-time
    78	// loss (lossPct). DefaultPaths leaves both zero, so Setup is unchanged.
    79	func SetupWithPaths(t *testing.T, paths []pathSpec) *Topology {
    80		t.Helper()
    81		top := &Topology{t: t, paths: paths}
    82	
    83		// Hold the concentrator network namespace open with a sleeping child.
    84		top.holder = exec.Command("unshare", "-n", "sleep", "600")
    85		if err := top.holder.Start(); err != nil {
    86			t.Fatalf("start concentrator netns holder: %v", err)
    87		}
    88		top.pid = top.holder.Process.Pid
    89		top.waitForNetns()
    90	
    91		pid := strconv.Itoa(top.pid)
    92		top.run("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up")
    93		top.nsenter("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up")
    94		// Pin net.ipv4.ip_nonlocal_bind=0 in the edge (test-process) network namespace, so
    95		// a path whose source_addr is NOT assigned to any interface deterministically fails
    96		// to bind (EADDRNOTAVAIL) instead of succeeding. That non-local-bind FAILURE is the
    97		// premise the tolerant-startup tests (T60) rest on: a not-yet-assignable source_addr
    98		// must DEFER (T51) and a config where NO path can bind must fail Open. A host that
    99		// leaves the sysctl at 1 (or runs permissively) lets a non-local bind SUCCEED, which
   100		// silently voids both premises (observed nondeterministic on a real host: a deferred
   101		// path binding instead of deferring, or a zero-bindable daemon coming up). A fresh
   102		// netns SHOULD default to 0, but pin it explicitly rather than trust the default.
   103		// This affects ONLY non-local binds, so every other e2e test — which binds real,
   104		// assigned addresses — is byte-for-byte unaffected. The concentrator netns needs no
   105		// pin: every concentrator source_addr (concIP) IS assigned there (deferEdgeAddr only
   106		// withholds the EDGE address), so the concentrator never binds a non-local address.
   107		disableNonlocalBind(top.t)
   108		for _, p := range top.paths {
   109			// Idempotent pre-delete: the veth names are FIXED per path, so a prior subtest's
   110			// teardown racing the kernel's async netns/veth reap can leave the edge veth behind,
   111			// making `ip link add` fail with EEXIST ("File exists"). Deleting the pair first
   112			// (ignore-if-absent) makes Setup robust to reused fixed names across sequential
   113			// subtests. Deleting the edge end removes its peer too.
   114			_ = top.tryRun("ip", "link", "del", p.edgeVeth)
   115			top.run("ip", "link", "add", p.edgeVeth, "type", "veth", "peer", "name", p.concVeth)
   116			top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.concVeth, "netns", pid)
   117			if !p.deferEdgeAddr {
   118				top.run("ip", "addr", "add", p.edgeIP+"/24", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
   119			}
   120			top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.edgeVeth, "up")
   121			top.nsenter("ip", "addr", "add", p.concIP+"/24", "dev", p.concVeth)
   122			top.nsenter("ip", "link", "set", p.concVeth, "up")
   123			qargs := append([]string{"qdisc", "add", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "root", "netem"}, top.netemArgs(p)...)
   124			top.run("tc", qargs...)
   125		}
   126		t.Cleanup(top.Teardown)
   127		return top
   128	}
   129	
   130	// waitForNetns blocks until the holder's network namespace is observable.
   131	func (top *Topology) waitForNetns() {
   132		path := fmt.Sprintf("/proc/%d/ns/net", top.pid)
   133		for i := 0; i < 100; i++ {
   134			if _, err := os.Stat(path); err == nil {
   135				return
   136			}
   137			time.Sleep(10 * time.Millisecond)
   138		}
   139		top.t.Fatalf("concentrator netns %s never appeared", path)
   140	}
   141	
   142	// netemArgs builds the netem parameter list for a path's baseline impairment
   143	// profile: delay/jitter, then its configured loss (lossPct) and bandwidth cap
   144	// (rateMbit) if set. For a DefaultPaths entry (both zero) this reduces to the
   145	// prior delay/jitter-only output, so existing qdisc setup is byte-identical.
   146	func (top *Topology) netemArgs(p pathSpec) []string {
   147		return top.netemArgsWithLoss(p, p.lossPct)
   148	}
   149	
   150	// netemArgsWithLoss is netemArgs with the loss percentage overridden — used by
   151	// InjectLoss to set runtime loss while preserving delay/jitter and any rate cap.
   152	// netem accepts the options in delay/loss/rate order.
   153	func (top *Topology) netemArgsWithLoss(p pathSpec, lossPct float64) []string {
   154		args := []string{"delay", fmt.Sprintf("%dms", p.delayMs)}
   155		if p.jitterMs > 0 {
   156			args = append(args, fmt.Sprintf("%dms", p.jitterMs))
   157		}
   158		if lossPct > 0 {
   159			args = append(args, "loss", fmt.Sprintf("%g%%", lossPct))
   160		}
   161		if p.rateMbit > 0 {
   162			args = append(args, "rate", fmt.Sprintf("%dmbit", p.rateMbit))
   163		}
   164		return args
   165	}
   166	
   167	// path looks up a path by name, failing the test if absent.
   168	func (top *Topology) path(name string) pathSpec {
   169		for _, p := range top.paths {
   170			if p.name == name {
   171				return p
   172			}
   173		}
   174		top.t.Fatalf("unknown path %q", name)
   175		return pathSpec{}
   176	}
   177	
   178	// RTT pings the concentrator end of the named path from the edge and returns the
   179	// average round-trip time in milliseconds.
   180	func (top *Topology) RTT(name string, count int) float64 {
   181		p := top.path(name)
   182		out := top.runOut("ping", "-c", strconv.Itoa(count), "-i", "0.2", "-W", "1", p.concIP)
   183		// rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 37.264/48.520/82.043/19.354 ms
   184		idx := strings.Index(out, "min/avg/max")
   185		if idx < 0 {
   186			top.t.Fatalf("path %q: no rtt line in ping output:\n%s", name, out)
   187		}
   188		eq := strings.Index(out[idx:], "=")
   189		fields := strings.Fields(out[idx+eq+1:])
   190		nums := strings.Split(fields[0], "/")
   191		avg, err := strconv.ParseFloat(nums[1], 64)
   192		if err != nil {
   193			top.t.Fatalf("path %q: parse avg rtt %q: %v", name, nums[1], err)
   194		}
   195		return avg
   196	}
   197	
   198	// Reachable reports whether the concentrator end of the named path answers pings.
   199	func (top *Topology) Reachable(name string, count int) bool {
   200		p := top.path(name)
   201		return top.tryRun("ping", "-c", strconv.Itoa(count), "-i", "0.2", "-W", "1", p.concIP) == nil
   202	}
   203	
   204	// InjectLoss sets uniform egress loss (percent) on the named path at runtime,
   205	// preserving its delay/jitter profile and any configured bandwidth cap.
   206	func (top *Topology) InjectLoss(name string, pct float64) {
   207		p := top.path(name)
   208		args := append([]string{"qdisc", "change", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "root", "netem"}, top.netemArgsWithLoss(p, pct)...)
   209		top.run("tc", args...)
   210	}
   211	
   212	// ClearLoss restores the named path to its configured baseline impairment
   213	// (delay/jitter, plus any config-time loss and bandwidth cap). For a DefaultPaths
   214	// entry that is delay/jitter only.
   215	func (top *Topology) ClearLoss(name string) {
   216		p := top.path(name)
   217		args := append([]string{"qdisc", "change", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "root", "netem"}, top.netemArgs(p)...)
   218		top.run("tc", args...)
   219	}
   220	
   221	// Blackhole brings the named path's edge link down, simulating a WAN death (used
   222	// by the P1 failover test).
   223	func (top *Topology) Blackhole(name string) {
   224		p := top.path(name)
   225		top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.edgeVeth, "down")
   226	}
   227	
   228	// Restore brings the named path's edge link back up.
   229	func (top *Topology) Restore(name string) {
   230		p := top.path(name)
   231		top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.edgeVeth, "up")
   232	}
   233	
   234	// Readdress replaces the edge-side address of the named path, simulating an edge
   235	// public-IP change (used by the P1 roaming test). newEdgeIP must be a bare IPv4
   236	// address in the path's /24.
   237	func (top *Topology) Readdress(name, newEdgeIP string) {
   238		p := top.path(name)
   239		top.run("ip", "addr", "flush", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
   240		top.run("ip", "addr", "add", newEdgeIP+"/24", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
   241		top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.edgeVeth, "up")
   242	}
   243	
   244	// AddEdgeAddr adds the named path's configured edge-side address to its (already up)
   245	// edge veth, WITHOUT flushing any existing address first (T60). It is the companion
   246	// to a path built with deferEdgeAddr: that path's interface starts addressless, so
   247	// this simulates the address becoming assignable after boot (e.g. a 5G modem's DHCP
   248	// lease completing) for the T55 background reconciler to observe. Unlike Readdress —
   249	// which flushes THEN re-adds, for the T16 re-roam case where an address is replaced —
   250	// this only ADDS, because here there is nothing to flush.
   251	func (top *Topology) AddEdgeAddr(name string) {
   252		p := top.path(name)
   253		top.run("ip", "addr", "add", p.edgeIP+"/24", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
   254	}
   255	
   256	// nonlocalBindProc is the per-netns sysctl controlling whether a socket may bind a
   257	// source address that is NOT assigned to any local interface. 0 (the value the
   258	// tolerant-startup tests pin) makes such a bind fail EADDRNOTAVAIL; 1 lets it
   259	// succeed. It is a plain proc file, so it is written/read directly rather than via
   260	// the `sysctl` binary (one fewer external dependency).
   261	const nonlocalBindProc = "/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_nonlocal_bind"
   262	
   263	// disableNonlocalBind pins ip_nonlocal_bind=0 in the CURRENT (edge/test) network
   264	// namespace and asserts the value took, failing the test loudly if it cannot be set.
   265	// A hard failure here — rather than proceeding — is deliberate: the tolerant-startup
   266	// premise (a non-local bind must fail) is silently voided if the sysctl stays at 1,
   267	// so an un-pinnable environment must surface as a test failure, never a false pass.
   268	// os.WriteFile targets THIS process's netns, which is the edge/test namespace (the
   269	// TestMain re-exec unshared it), exactly where the not-yet-assignable source_addr
   270	// binds live. Writing a per-netns net sysctl needs the same privilege the fixture's
   271	// veth/netem setup already requires, so anywhere the netns tier runs, this succeeds.
   272	func disableNonlocalBind(t *testing.T) {
   273		t.Helper()
   274		if err := os.WriteFile(nonlocalBindProc, []byte("0\n"), 0o644); err != nil {
   275			t.Fatalf("pin %s=0: %v (the tolerant-startup tests need a non-local bind to fail EADDRNOTAVAIL; without this a permissive host silently voids the premise)", nonlocalBindProc, err)
   276		}
   277		got, err := os.ReadFile(nonlocalBindProc)
   278		if err != nil {
   279			t.Fatalf("read back %s: %v", nonlocalBindProc, err)
   280		}
   281		if v := strings.TrimSpace(string(got)); v != "0" {
   282			t.Fatalf("%s = %q after pin, want 0 — a non-local bind would still succeed and void the zero-bindable/deferred premise", nonlocalBindProc, v)
   283		}
   284	}
   285	
   286	// bringLoopbackUp brings the loopback interface UP in the CURRENT (edge/test)
   287	// network namespace. It is a second, INDEPENDENT precondition for a non-local bind
   288	// to fail EADDRNOTAVAIL: on this kernel the rejection fires only when at least one
   289	// interface is UP — empirically, with lo DOWN a bind to a non-local address SUCCEEDS
   290	// even at ip_nonlocal_bind=0 (kernel probe: lo-up+pin0 -> EADDRNOTAVAIL; lo-down+pin0
   291	// -> BIND_OK; lo-up+pin1 -> BIND_OK). So the zero-bindable premise needs BOTH lo UP
   292	// and the sysctl pinned to 0. SetupWithPaths already brings lo up for topology tests
   293	// (line above), which is why the zero-bindable subtest passed only when it happened to
   294	// run AFTER one; building no topology itself, it must bring lo up explicitly to hold
   295	// regardless of subtest execution order. Idempotent (setting an already-up lo up is a
   296	// no-op). Runs in this process's netns (the TestMain re-exec unshared it), like run().
   297	func bringLoopbackUp(t *testing.T) {
   298		t.Helper()
   299		if out, err := exec.Command("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up").CombinedOutput(); err != nil {
   300			t.Fatalf("bring lo up in the test netns: %v\n%s", err, out)
   301		}
   302	}

   304	// QdiscShow returns `tc qdisc show` for the named path (for assertions/debug).
   305	func (top *Topology) QdiscShow(name string) string {
   306		p := top.path(name)
   307		return top.runOut("tc", "qdisc", "show", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
   308	}

   310	// LinkExists reports whether the edge-side veth of the named path still exists.
   311	func (top *Topology) LinkExists(name string) bool {
   312		p := top.path(name)
   313		return top.tryRun("ip", "link", "show", p.edgeVeth) == nil
   314	}

   316	// Teardown removes the topology. Killing the holder drops the concentrator netns
   317	// (and its veth ends); the edge-side veths are deleted explicitly. Idempotent.
   318	func (top *Topology) Teardown() {
   319		for _, p := range top.paths {
   320			_ = top.tryRun("ip", "link", "del", p.edgeVeth)
   321		}
   322		if top.holder != nil && top.holder.Process != nil {
   323			_ = top.holder.Process.Kill()
   324			_, _ = top.holder.Process.Wait()
   325			top.holder = nil
   326		}
   327	}
   328	
   329	func (top *Topology) run(name string, args ...string) {
   330		top.t.Helper()
   331		if out, err := exec.Command(name, args...).CombinedOutput(); err != nil {
   332			top.t.Fatalf("%s %s: %v\n%s", name, strings.Join(args, " "), err, out)
   333		}
   334	}
   335	
   336	func (top *Topology) tryRun(name string, args ...string) error {
   337		return exec.Command(name, args...).Run()
   338	}
   339	
   340	func (top *Topology) runOut(name string, args ...string) string {
   341		top.t.Helper()
   342		out, err := exec.Command(name, args...).CombinedOutput()
   343		if err != nil {
   344			top.t.Fatalf("%s %s: %v\n%s", name, strings.Join(args, " "), err, out)
   345		}
   346		return string(out)
   347	}
   348	
   349	// iperfListenTimeout bounds waitIperfListen: how long to wait for the one-shot
   350	// iperf3 server to reach the LISTEN state before failing the test.
   351	const iperfListenTimeout = 5 * time.Second
   352	
   353	// waitIperfListen polls, inside the concentrator network namespace, for a TCP
   354	// socket in the LISTEN state on the given port, and returns once it appears (or
   355	// fails the test at iperfListenTimeout). It reads kernel socket state via
   356	// `ss -ltn` and NEVER connects: the iperf3 servers here run one-shot (`-s -1`),
   357	// so a probe-connect would consume the server's single accept and break the real
   358	// client. It replaces the prior fixed sleeps at the iperf3Mbps and rttUnderLoad
   359	// call sites, which raced a slow bind under load into 'connection refused' (D3).
   360	func (top *Topology) waitIperfListen(t *testing.T, port int) {
   361		t.Helper()
   362		suffix := fmt.Sprintf(":%d", port)
   363		deadline := time.Now().Add(iperfListenTimeout)
   364		for time.Now().Before(deadline) {
   365			// ss -ltn columns: State Recv-Q Send-Q Local-Address:Port Peer-Address:Port.
   366			out, err := exec.Command("nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", "ss", "-ltn").CombinedOutput()
   367			if err == nil {
   368				for _, line := range strings.Split(string(out), "\n") {
   369					fields := strings.Fields(line)
   370					if len(fields) >= 4 && strings.HasSuffix(fields[3], suffix) {
   371						return
   372					}
   373				}
   374			}
   375			time.Sleep(20 * time.Millisecond)
   376		}
   377		t.Fatalf("iperf3 server never reached LISTEN on port %d within %s", port, iperfListenTimeout)
   378	}
   379	
   380	// nsenter runs a command inside the concentrator network namespace.
   381	func (top *Topology) nsenter(args ...string) {
   382		top.t.Helper()
   383		full := append([]string{"-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n"}, args...)
   384		top.run("nsenter", full...)
   385	}

    15	// pathSpec describes one emulated WAN uplink between the edge and concentrator
    16	// namespaces: a veth pair carrying a /24, with netem delay+jitter on the edge
    17	// egress. The bandwidth cap (rateMbit) and controlled loss (lossPct) are
    18	// OPTIONAL config-time impairments: both default to zero, so the DefaultPaths
    19	// topology stays uncapped and lossless and every existing P0/P1 e2e test runs
    20	// unchanged. A non-zero rateMbit makes the LINK — not the single-core userspace
    21	// WG crypto — the bottleneck ONLY when rateMbit sits below the EXECUTING host's
    22	// measured in-fixture tunnel ceiling. That ceiling is CPU/PPS-bound (both daemons
    23	// plus the load generator share the host's cores), a lower bound that scales with
    24	// core count, NOT a link-throughput spec: ~12–46 Mbit/s single-flow on a 1-vCPU
    25	// aarch64 host (docs/p0-findings.md:216-225), ~13 Mbit/s single-path (up to
    26	// ~47–87 Mbit/s FEC single-flow) on a 4-vCPU amd64 host. Sizing rule: cap < ceiling
    27	// for single-path, 2×cap < ceiling for aggregation. Below the ceiling
    28	// a standing queue can form for bufferbloat/pacing (T21/T23) work; a non-zero
    29	// lossPct injects uniform egress loss at Setup time for FEC-recovery (T25/T29)
    30	// work.
    31	type pathSpec struct {
    32		name     string
    33		edgeIP   string
    34		concIP   string
    35		edgeVeth string // <=15 chars
    36		concVeth string
    37		delayMs  int
    38		jitterMs int
    39		rateMbit int     // optional per-path bandwidth cap (netem rate); 0 = uncapped
    40		lossPct  float64 // optional config-time uniform egress loss (netem loss); 0 = lossless
    41	
    42		// deferEdgeAddr, when true, makes Setup create and bring up the edge veth WITHOUT
    43		// assigning edgeIP to it: the interface exists (so tc/netem still applies and the
    44		// link is up) but the configured source_addr is not yet owned by any interface —
    45		// the well-formed-but-not-yet-assignable condition T51's tolerant Open() defers and
    46		// T55's background reconcile later promotes (T60). AddEdgeAddr adds the withheld
    47		// address later. Zero value (false) is the default for every existing path, so
    48		// Setup's behaviour for DefaultPaths and every other caller is unchanged.
    49		deferEdgeAddr bool
    50	}
    51	
    52	// DefaultPaths are the two emulated links: Starlink-like (low latency, jittery)
    53	// and 5G-like (higher latency, stable).
    54	var DefaultPaths = []pathSpec{
    55		{name: "starlink", edgeIP: "10.100.1.1", concIP: "10.100.1.2", edgeVeth: "wbAe", concVeth: "wbAc", delayMs: 45, jitterMs: 10},
    56		{name: "cellular", edgeIP: "10.100.2.1", concIP: "10.100.2.2", edgeVeth: "wbBe", concVeth: "wbBc", delayMs: 64, jitterMs: 0},
    57	}
    58	
    59	// Topology is a two-namespace netns/netem fixture. The edge side is the current
    60	// (re-exec'd) network namespace; the concentrator side is a child process's
    61	// network namespace, addressed by PID so no writable /run/netns is required.
    62	type Topology struct {
    63		t      *testing.T
    64		holder *exec.Cmd
    65		pid    int
    66		paths  []pathSpec
    67	}
    68	
    69	// Setup builds the two-path topology from DefaultPaths (uncapped, lossless). It
    70	// requires CAP_NET_ADMIN, which the e2e TestMain provides via an unprivileged
    71	// user+net namespace (`unshare -Urmn`).
    72	func Setup(t *testing.T) *Topology {
    73		return SetupWithPaths(t, DefaultPaths)
    74	}
    75	
    76	// SetupWithPaths builds the topology from an explicit path set, allowing a test
    77	// to opt into the optional per-path bandwidth cap (rateMbit) and/or config-time
    78	// loss (lossPct). DefaultPaths leaves both zero, so Setup is unchanged.
    79	func SetupWithPaths(t *testing.T, paths []pathSpec) *Topology {
    80		t.Helper()
    81		top := &Topology{t: t, paths: paths}
    82	
    83		// Hold the concentrator network namespace open with a sleeping child.
    84		top.holder = exec.Command("unshare", "-n", "sleep", "600")
    85		if err := top.holder.Start(); err != nil {
    86			t.Fatalf("start concentrator netns holder: %v", err)
    87		}
    88		top.pid = top.holder.Process.Pid
    89		top.waitForNetns()
    90	
    91		pid := strconv.Itoa(top.pid)
    92		top.run("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up")
    93		top.nsenter("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up")
    94		// Pin net.ipv4.ip_nonlocal_bind=0 in the edge (test-process) network namespace, so
    95		// a path whose source_addr is NOT assigned to any interface deterministically fails
    96		// to bind (EADDRNOTAVAIL) instead of succeeding. That non-local-bind FAILURE is the
    97		// premise the tolerant-startup tests (T60) rest on: a not-yet-assignable source_addr
    98		// must DEFER (T51) and a config where NO path can bind must fail Open. A host that
    99		// leaves the sysctl at 1 (or runs permissively) lets a non-local bind SUCCEED, which
   100		// silently voids both premises (observed nondeterministic on a real host: a deferred
   101		// path binding instead of deferring, or a zero-bindable daemon coming up). A fresh
   102		// netns SHOULD default to 0, but pin it explicitly rather than trust the default.
   103		// This affects ONLY non-local binds, so every other e2e test — which binds real,
   104		// assigned addresses — is byte-for-byte unaffected. The concentrator netns needs no
   105		// pin: every concentrator source_addr (concIP) IS assigned there (deferEdgeAddr only
   106		// withholds the EDGE address), so the concentrator never binds a non-local address.
   107		disableNonlocalBind(top.t)
   108		for _, p := range top.paths {
   109			// Idempotent pre-delete: the veth names are FIXED per path, so a prior subtest's
   110			// teardown racing the kernel's async netns/veth reap can leave the edge veth behind,
   111			// making `ip link add` fail with EEXIST ("File exists"). Deleting the pair first
   112			// (ignore-if-absent) makes Setup robust to reused fixed names across sequential
   113			// subtests. Deleting the edge end removes its peer too.
   114			_ = top.tryRun("ip", "link", "del", p.edgeVeth)
   115			top.run("ip", "link", "add", p.edgeVeth, "type", "veth", "peer", "name", p.concVeth)
   116			top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.concVeth, "netns", pid)
   117			if !p.deferEdgeAddr {
   118				top.run("ip", "addr", "add", p.edgeIP+"/24", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
   119			}
   120			top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.edgeVeth, "up")
   121			top.nsenter("ip", "addr", "add", p.concIP+"/24", "dev", p.concVeth)
   122			top.nsenter("ip", "link", "set", p.concVeth, "up")
   123			qargs := append([]string{"qdisc", "add", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "root", "netem"}, top.netemArgs(p)...)
   124			top.run("tc", qargs...)
   125		}
   126		t.Cleanup(top.Teardown)
   127		return top
   128	}

   130	// waitForNetns blocks until the holder's network namespace is observable.
   131	func (top *Topology) waitForNetns() {
   132		path := fmt.Sprintf("/proc/%d/ns/net", top.pid)
   133		for i := 0; i < 100; i++ {
   134			if _, err := os.Stat(path); err == nil {
   135				return
   136			}
   137			time.Sleep(10 * time.Millisecond)
   138		}
   139		top.t.Fatalf("concentrator netns %s never appeared", path)
   140	}
   141	
   142	// netemArgs builds the netem parameter list for a path's baseline impairment
   143	// profile: delay/jitter, then its configured loss (lossPct) and bandwidth cap
   144	// (rateMbit) if set. For a DefaultPaths entry (both zero) this reduces to the
   145	// prior delay/jitter-only output, so existing qdisc setup is byte-identical.
   146	func (top *Topology) netemArgs(p pathSpec) []string {
   147		return top.netemArgsWithLoss(p, p.lossPct)
   148	}
   149	
   150	// netemArgsWithLoss is netemArgs with the loss percentage overridden — used by
   151	// InjectLoss to set runtime loss while preserving delay/jitter and any rate cap.
   152	// netem accepts the options in delay/loss/rate order.
   153	func (top *Topology) netemArgsWithLoss(p pathSpec, lossPct float64) []string {
   154		args := []string{"delay", fmt.Sprintf("%dms", p.delayMs)}
   155		if p.jitterMs > 0 {
   156			args = append(args, fmt.Sprintf("%dms", p.jitterMs))
   157		}
   158		if lossPct > 0 {
   159			args = append(args, "loss", fmt.Sprintf("%g%%", lossPct))
   160		}
   161		if p.rateMbit > 0 {
   162			args = append(args, "rate", fmt.Sprintf("%dmbit", p.rateMbit))
   163		}
   164		return args
   165	}

   167	// path looks up a path by name, failing the test if absent.
   168	func (top *Topology) path(name string) pathSpec {
   169		for _, p := range top.paths {
   170			if p.name == name {
   171				return p
   172			}
   173		}
   174		top.t.Fatalf("unknown path %q", name)
   175		return pathSpec{}
   176	}

   178	// RTT pings the concentrator end of the named path from the edge and returns the
   179	// average round-trip time in milliseconds.
   180	func (top *Topology) RTT(name string, count int) float64 {
   181		p := top.path(name)
   182		out := top.runOut("ping", "-c", strconv.Itoa(count), "-i", "0.2", "-W", "1", p.concIP)
   183		// rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 37.264/48.520/82.043/19.354 ms
   184		idx := strings.Index(out, "min/avg/max")
   185		if idx < 0 {
   186			top.t.Fatalf("path %q: no rtt line in ping output:\n%s", name, out)
   187		}
   188		eq := strings.Index(out[idx:], "=")
   189		fields := strings.Fields(out[idx+eq+1:])
   190		nums := strings.Split(fields[0], "/")
   191		avg, err := strconv.ParseFloat(nums[1], 64)
   192		if err != nil {
   193			top.t.Fatalf("path %q: parse avg rtt %q: %v", name, nums[1], err)
   194		}
   195		return avg
   196	}

   251	func (top *Topology) AddEdgeAddr(name string) {

   256	// nonlocalBindProc is the per-netns sysctl controlling whether a socket may bind a

   260	// the `sysctl` binary (one fewer external dependency).

   263	// disableNonlocalBind pins ip_nonlocal_bind=0 in the CURRENT (edge/test) network

   271	// veth/netem setup already requires, so anywhere the netns tier runs, this succeeds.
   272	func disableNonlocalBind(t *testing.T) {

   288	// to fail EADDRNOTAVAIL: on this kernel the rejection fires only when at least one

   293	// (line above), which is why the zero-bindable subtest passed only when it happened to

   295	// regardless of subtest execution order. Idempotent (setting an already-up lo up is a

   297	func bringLoopbackUp(t *testing.T) {

   310	// LinkExists reports whether the edge-side veth of the named path still exists.
   311	func (top *Topology) LinkExists(name string) bool {
   312		p := top.path(name)
   313		return top.tryRun("ip", "link", "show", p.edgeVeth) == nil
   314	}
   315	
   316	// Teardown removes the topology. Killing the holder drops the concentrator netns
   317	// (and its veth ends); the edge-side veths are deleted explicitly. Idempotent.
   318	func (top *Topology) Teardown() {
   319		for _, p := range top.paths {
   320			_ = top.tryRun("ip", "link", "del", p.edgeVeth)
   321		}
   322		if top.holder != nil && top.holder.Process != nil {
   323			_ = top.holder.Process.Kill()
   324			_, _ = top.holder.Process.Wait()
   325			top.holder = nil
   326		}
   327	}
   328	
   329	func (top *Topology) run(name string, args ...string) {
   330		top.t.Helper()
   331		if out, err := exec.Command(name, args...).CombinedOutput(); err != nil {
   332			top.t.Fatalf("%s %s: %v\n%s", name, strings.Join(args, " "), err, out)
   333		}
   334	}
   335	
   336	func (top *Topology) tryRun(name string, args ...string) error {
   337		return exec.Command(name, args...).Run()
   338	}
   339	
   340	func (top *Topology) runOut(name string, args ...string) string {
   341		top.t.Helper()
   342		out, err := exec.Command(name, args...).CombinedOutput()
   343		if err != nil {
   344			top.t.Fatalf("%s %s: %v\n%s", name, strings.Join(args, " "), err, out)
   345		}
   346		return string(out)
   347	}
   348	
   349	// iperfListenTimeout bounds waitIperfListen: how long to wait for the one-shot
   350	// iperf3 server to reach the LISTEN state before failing the test.
   351	const iperfListenTimeout = 5 * time.Second
   352	
   353	// waitIperfListen polls, inside the concentrator network namespace, for a TCP
   354	// socket in the LISTEN state on the given port, and returns once it appears (or
   355	// fails the test at iperfListenTimeout). It reads kernel socket state via
   356	// `ss -ltn` and NEVER connects: the iperf3 servers here run one-shot (`-s -1`),
   357	// so a probe-connect would consume the server's single accept and break the real
   358	// client. It replaces the prior fixed sleeps at the iperf3Mbps and rttUnderLoad
   359	// call sites, which raced a slow bind under load into 'connection refused' (D3).
   360	func (top *Topology) waitIperfListen(t *testing.T, port int) {
   361		t.Helper()
   362		suffix := fmt.Sprintf(":%d", port)
   363		deadline := time.Now().Add(iperfListenTimeout)
   364		for time.Now().Before(deadline) {
   365			// ss -ltn columns: State Recv-Q Send-Q Local-Address:Port Peer-Address:Port.
   366			out, err := exec.Command("nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", "ss", "-ltn").CombinedOutput()
   367			if err == nil {
   368				for _, line := range strings.Split(string(out), "\n") {
   369					fields := strings.Fields(line)
   370					if len(fields) >= 4 && strings.HasSuffix(fields[3], suffix) {
   371						return
   372					}
   373				}
   374			}
   375			time.Sleep(20 * time.Millisecond)
   376		}
   377		t.Fatalf("iperf3 server never reached LISTEN on port %d within %s", port, iperfListenTimeout)
   378	}
   379	
   380	// nsenter runs a command inside the concentrator network namespace.
   381	func (top *Topology) nsenter(args ...string) {
   382		top.t.Helper()
   383		full := append([]string{"-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n"}, args...)
   384		top.run("nsenter", full...)
   385	}

    16	// is what makes an edge's path survive a public-IP change mid-session (a re-roam

    18	// socket keeps sending, now sourced from the interface's NEW address, and the far

    22	// without a re-Open (which would reset the WireGuard session).

    39		}

     4	
     5	//go:build linux
     6	
     7	package unix
     8	
     9	import (
    10		"unsafe"
    11	)
    12	
    13	// Helpers for dealing with ifreq since it contains a union and thus requires a
    14	// lot of unsafe.Pointer casts to use properly.
    15	
    16	// An Ifreq is a type-safe wrapper around the raw ifreq struct. An Ifreq
    17	// contains an interface name and a union of arbitrary data which can be
    18	// accessed using the Ifreq's methods. To create an Ifreq, use the NewIfreq
    19	// function.
    20	//
    21	// Use the Name method to access the stored interface name. The union data
    22	// fields can be get and set using the following methods:
    23	//   - Uint16/SetUint16: flags
    24	//   - Uint32/SetUint32: ifindex, metric, mtu
    25	type Ifreq struct{ raw ifreq }
    26	
    27	// NewIfreq creates an Ifreq with the input network interface name after
    28	// validating the name does not exceed IFNAMSIZ-1 (trailing NULL required)
    29	// bytes.
    30	func NewIfreq(name string) (*Ifreq, error) {
    31		// Leave room for terminating NULL byte.
    32		if len(name) >= IFNAMSIZ {
    33			return nil, EINVAL
    34		}
    35	
    36		var ifr ifreq
    37		copy(ifr.Ifrn[:], name)
    38	
    39		return &Ifreq{raw: ifr}, nil
    40	}
    41	
    42	// TODO(mdlayher): get/set methods for hardware address sockaddr, char array, etc.
    43	
    44	// Name returns the interface name associated with the Ifreq.
    45	func (ifr *Ifreq) Name() string {
    46		return ByteSliceToString(ifr.raw.Ifrn[:])
    47	}
    48	
    49	// According to netdevice(7), only AF_INET addresses are returned for numerous
    50	// sockaddr ioctls. For convenience, we expose these as Inet4Addr since the Port
    51	// field and other data is always empty.
    52	
    53	// Inet4Addr returns the Ifreq union data from an embedded sockaddr as a C
    54	// in_addr/Go []byte (4-byte IPv4 address) value. If the sockaddr family is not
    55	// AF_INET, an error is returned.
    56	func (ifr *Ifreq) Inet4Addr() ([]byte, error) {
    57		raw := *(*RawSockaddrInet4)(unsafe.Pointer(&ifr.raw.Ifru[:SizeofSockaddrInet4][0]))
    58		if raw.Family != AF_INET {
    59			// Cannot safely interpret raw.Addr bytes as an IPv4 address.
    60			return nil, EINVAL
    61		}
    62	
    63		return raw.Addr[:], nil
    64	}
    65	
    66	// SetInet4Addr sets a C in_addr/Go []byte (4-byte IPv4 address) value in an
    67	// embedded sockaddr within the Ifreq's union data. v must be 4 bytes in length
    68	// or an error will be returned.
    69	func (ifr *Ifreq) SetInet4Addr(v []byte) error {
    70		if len(v) != 4 {
    71			return EINVAL
    72		}
    73	
    74		var addr [4]byte
    75		copy(addr[:], v)
    76	
    77		ifr.clear()
    78		*(*RawSockaddrInet4)(
    79			unsafe.Pointer(&ifr.raw.Ifru[:SizeofSockaddrInet4][0]),
    80		) = RawSockaddrInet4{
    81			// Always set IP family as ioctls would require it anyway.
    82			Family: AF_INET,
    83			Addr:   addr,
    84		}
    85	
    86		return nil
    87	}
    88	
    89	// Uint16 returns the Ifreq union data as a C short/Go uint16 value.
    90	func (ifr *Ifreq) Uint16() uint16 {
    91		return *(*uint16)(unsafe.Pointer(&ifr.raw.Ifru[:2][0]))
    92	}
    93	
    94	// SetUint16 sets a C short/Go uint16 value as the Ifreq's union data.
    95	func (ifr *Ifreq) SetUint16(v uint16) {
    96		ifr.clear()
    97		*(*uint16)(unsafe.Pointer(&ifr.raw.Ifru[:2][0])) = v
    98	}
    99	
   100	// Uint32 returns the Ifreq union data as a C int/Go uint32 value.
   101	func (ifr *Ifreq) Uint32() uint32 {
   102		return *(*uint32)(unsafe.Pointer(&ifr.raw.Ifru[:4][0]))
   103	}
   104	
   105	// SetUint32 sets a C int/Go uint32 value as the Ifreq's union data.
   106	func (ifr *Ifreq) SetUint32(v uint32) {
   107		ifr.clear()
   108		*(*uint32)(unsafe.Pointer(&ifr.raw.Ifru[:4][0])) = v
   109	}
   110	
   111	// clear zeroes the ifreq's union field to prevent trailing garbage data from
   112	// being sent to the kernel if an ifreq is reused.
   113	func (ifr *Ifreq) clear() {
   114		for i := range ifr.raw.Ifru {
   115			ifr.raw.Ifru[i] = 0
   116		}
   117	}
   118	
   119	// TODO(mdlayher): export as IfreqData? For now we can provide helpers such as
   120	// IoctlGetEthtoolDrvinfo which use these APIs under the hood.
   121	
   122	// An ifreqData is an Ifreq which carries pointer data. To produce an ifreqData,
   123	// use the Ifreq.withData method.
   124	type ifreqData struct {
   125		name [IFNAMSIZ]byte
   126		// A type separate from ifreq is required in order to comply with the
   127		// unsafe.Pointer rules since the "pointer-ness" of data would not be
   128		// preserved if it were cast into the byte array of a raw ifreq.
   129		data unsafe.Pointer
   130		// Pad to the same size as ifreq.
   131		_ [len(ifreq{}.Ifru) - SizeofPtr]byte
   132	}
   133	
   134	// withData produces an ifreqData with the pointer p set for ioctls which require
   135	// arbitrary pointer data.
   136	func (ifr Ifreq) withData(p unsafe.Pointer) ifreqData {
   137		return ifreqData{
   138			name: ifr.raw.Ifrn,
   139			data: p,
   140		}
   141	}

# wanbond task runner. Targets assume the dev shell is active (`nix develop`),
# which puts go, golangci-lint, and the netem/DPI tooling on PATH.

# Show available targets.
default:
    @just --list

# Build all non-privileged packages.
build:
    go build ./...

# Cross-compile static release binaries (CGO_ENABLED=0, stripped, version
# stamped from git) for linux/amd64 and linux/arm64 into dist/. `file` reports
# them statically linked; see docs/install.md for the deployment steps.
release:
    mkdir -p dist
    CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=amd64 go build -trimpath -ldflags "-s -w -X main.version=$(git describe --tags --always --dirty)" -o dist/wanbond-linux-amd64 ./cmd/wanbond
    CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux GOARCH=arm64 go build -trimpath -ldflags "-s -w -X main.version=$(git describe --tags --always --dirty)" -o dist/wanbond-linux-arm64 ./cmd/wanbond

# The e2e/realhosts sources are gated behind //go:build tags, so a tagless
# vet/lint never compiles them and silently skips them (see D28). The tagged
# passes below are additive — the tagless run still analyses the default build
# exactly as before.
# Vet + lint the default build plus the e2e- and realhosts-tagged sources.
lint:
    go vet ./...
    go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
    go vet -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/...
    golangci-lint run
    golangci-lint run --build-tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
    golangci-lint run --build-tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/...

# Format check (fails if anything is unformatted).
fmt-check:
    test -z "$(gofmt -l cmd internal test)"

# Unprivileged unit/property tests only (the e2e build tag is never set here, so
# no privileged test compiles or runs).
test:
    go test ./...

# Privileged end-to-end suite: netns/netem + TUN tunnel bring-up. Requires root
# (CAP_NET_ADMIN + /dev/net/tun); run on real or emulated hardware, not CI.
e2e:
    sudo -E go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e/... -count=1

# Run one named e2e phase test, e.g. `just e2e-run TestP1Failover`.
e2e-run TEST:
    sudo -E go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e/... -run {{TEST}} -count=1 -v

# Opt-in real-host e2e tier: drive the two standing worker machines (edge behind
# symmetric NAT, aarch64 concentrator) over SSH. Report-only — it records host
# uname/arch and gates nothing. Needs the `llm` SSH key (WANBOND_SSH_KEY, default
# /run/agenix/llm-ssh-key); no root required. NEVER part of `just test` or CI.
# Run all: `just realhosts`; one test: `just realhosts TestRealConnectivity`.
realhosts TEST="":
    go test -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/... {{ if TEST != "" { "-run " + TEST } else { "" } }} -count=1 -v

# Opt-in idempotent real-host provisioning: ensure iperf3 + gcc + Go 1.26.x on
# both hosts and the concentrator's tunnel-interface INPUT ACCEPT rule. Report-
# only, idempotent (a second run reports no changes); same SSH key requirements
# as `realhosts`. Thin wrapper over TestRealProvision. NEVER part of `just test`.
realhosts-provision:
    go test -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/... -run TestRealProvision -count=1 -v

# P0 real-link baseline: the SINGLE repeatable pre-pilot procedure (docs/manual-
# checklist.md §P0). A thin orchestration layer over three EXISTING realhosts
# tests — it provisions both ends, brings the tunnel up, and records:
#   - TestRealP0Smoke              single-uplink handshake + ping RTT + iperf3
#                                  (single/8x-parallel TCP, UDP goodput/loss);
#   - TestRealAggregationBufferbloat  per-path + bonded throughput and their
#                                  aggregation ratio, plus idle-vs-loaded RTT
#                                  bufferbloat delta under saturating load;
#   - TestRealMidTransferWANKill   mid-transfer LINK failover and HUB failover
#                                  (T57) recovery timings.
# It TEES the full -v output to a timestamped baseline report under
# test/realhosts/reports/ (gitignored). REPORT-ONLY (Q19): the underlying tests
# assert LIVENESS ONLY — no Mbit/s or ms threshold gates the run; the numbers are
# informational for the operator's (non-blocking) pilot-gate decision. Same SSH
# key requirement as `realhosts` (WANBOND_SSH_KEY, default /run/agenix/llm-ssh-
# key). NEVER part of `just test` or CI. A non-zero exit means the run itself
# could not complete (host unreachable, tunnel never came up), NOT that a
# performance number missed a target.
p0-baseline:
    #!/usr/bin/env bash
    set -euo pipefail
    mkdir -p test/realhosts/reports
    report="test/realhosts/reports/p0-baseline-$(date -u +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ).log"
    echo "wanbond P0 real-link baseline — $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)" | tee "$report"
    go test -tags realhosts ./test/realhosts/... \
        -run '^(TestRealP0Smoke|TestRealAggregationBufferbloat|TestRealMidTransferWANKill)$' \
        -count=1 -v 2>&1 | tee -a "$report"
    echo "baseline report: $report" | tee -a "$report"

# Agent instructions — wanbond

Instructions for AI agents (and humans) working in this repo. Read the
[README](README.md) for what wanbond is and [docs/design.md](docs/design.md) for
the architecture before changing anything.

## Keep the docs current (required)

**Documentation is part of the definition of done.** When a change alters
behavior, configuration, architecture, the wire format, an invariant, or an
operational step, update the affected docs **in the same change**:

- **[README.md](README.md)** — the front door: capabilities, quick start, repo
  layout, status/limitations. Update when a capability, config surface, package,
  or limitation changes.
- **[docs/design.md](docs/design.md)** — architecture and what we built on top of
  amneziawg-go. Update when the data path, a layer/package, an invariant, the
  security model, or a deliberate boundary changes.
- **[docs/install.md](docs/install.md)** — setup and operation. Update when
  config keys, systemd units, firewall/provisioning steps, or metrics change.
- **[docs/manual-checklist.md](docs/manual-checklist.md)** — update when a
  verification step or a real-link measurement changes.

A change that moves the code but not the docs it invalidates is **incomplete**.
If you are unsure whether a doc is affected, grep it for the symbol/keyword you
touched. Prefer editing the existing doc over adding a new one; keep docs
consistent with each other (e.g. a config key described in README, design, and
install must match).

## Build, test, lint

The dev shell (`nix develop`) puts Go 1.26, golangci-lint, and the netem/DPI
tooling on `PATH`. Run tools from it (e.g. `nix develop --command bash -c '…'`).

```sh
just build      # go build ./...
just test       # unprivileged unit/property tests (go test ./...)
just lint       # go vet + golangci-lint, INCLUDING -tags e2e and -tags realhosts
just e2e        # privileged netns fixture: sudo -E go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
just realhosts  # opt-in real-machine tier (-tags realhosts), report-only
```

The non-privileged gate a change must pass before merge:

```sh
go build ./... && go vet ./... && test -z "$(gofmt -l cmd internal test)" && go test ./...
```

`-tags e2e` / `-tags realhosts` need root / real hosts and are **not** part of the
default gate; validate them separately (see Testing discipline).

## Load-bearing invariants — do not break

Full detail in [docs/design.md §Load-bearing invariants](docs/design.md). In
short:

1. The engine sees **one virtual endpoint per peer**; the Bind fans out beneath
   it (design rule A1). No per-packet endpoint churn to the engine.
2. Use wanbond's **own outer sequence space**; never reuse/perturb the inner
   WireGuard counter.
3. **Resequence before** the inner anti-replay window validates.
4. Inner fail-closed; **PROBE/CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated** with monotonic
   anti-replay; DATA/PARITY are unauthenticated **by design** (DoS-grade risk
   accepted — do not "fix" this without a design decision).
5. All engine (`conn`) coupling stays isolated to **`internal/bind/bind.go`** —
   preserve the fork-swap hedge.
6. Amnezia config is **all-or-nothing** and the engine is **single-per-process**
   (package-level globals). Keep the config validation that enforces this.
7. On **any `klauspost/reedsolomon` (or amneziawg-go) version bump**, re-verify
   `TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant` (`internal/fec`) before landing —
   a flipped default matrix silently corrupts every reconstructed payload.

## Testing discipline

- **Reproduce before fixing.** For a suspected defect, write a failing test (or a
  documented minimal repro) that fails for the *expected* reason *first*; then fix
  and confirm it passes. Assertions must be non-vacuous — a test that cannot fail
  on the unfixed code proves nothing (e.g. a goroutine-leak fix needs a
  `goleak`/`NumGoroutine` gate, not a bare `-count` run).
- **The netns fixture is CPU/PPS-bound.** It validates *functional* bonding,
  failover, FEC recovery, and DPI — **not** absolute throughput or bufferbloat.
  Do not assert link-throughput numbers from it; use counter ratios / functional
  checks. Real throughput belongs to the real-host tier / manual checklist.
- **Hardware-validate** changes that touch the netns fixture or real-host
  behavior on the standing worker machines (an amd64 NAT edge + an aarch64 public
  concentrator) before considering them done; these tiers are report-only and not
  in the default gate.
- Never commit code changes on a ledger/docs commit and vice versa; keep commits
  scoped.

## Conventions

- **Surgical changes.** Touch only what the task requires; match surrounding
  style; don't refactor unrelated code or "improve" adjacent comments.
- **Fail fast at boundaries** (config load, external input); no silent fallbacks
  for internal logic.
- **No new dependencies** without a clear reason; prefer the standard library and
  the already-vendored `amneziawg-go` / `klauspost/reedsolomon`.
- Keep new comments minimal and only for the non-obvious; don't delete correct
  existing comments.

## Planning ledger (cq)

This repo tracks work in a cq planning ledger under `.cq/` (goals, tasks,
defects, reviews, decisions). If you use it, record provenance
(`author`/`session`) on every write and keep ledger commits (`.cq/` only)
separate from code commits. It is optional for one-off changes.

### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)

The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and owns ONLY the tunnel
engine — **it never assigns addresses or routes** (wg-quick style, no
privileged shell-outs). Assign them with a **systemd-networkd `.network`
file**, using the inner addresses from `allowed_ips`:

```ini
# /etc/systemd/network/10-wanbond0.network  (edge; concentrator: 10.77.0.1/24)
[Match]
Name=wanbond0

[Network]
Address=10.77.0.2/24
```

```sh
systemctl enable --now systemd-networkd
```

Do **not** use a `[Service] ExecStartPost=ip address add … dev wanbond0`
drop-in: the units are `Type=exec`, so systemd considers the service started
the instant `execve()` returns — *before* the daemon has created `wanbond0` —
and the `ExecStartPost` would run against a not-yet-existent interface, fail
with "Cannot find device", and (being an un-prefixed `ExecStartPost`) fail the
unit and trip the `Restart=on-failure` crash-loop. networkd is race-free: it

## 5. Firewall

### Concentrator: UDP listen port

Allow the WireGuard UDP port in from the WAN (and, on OCI, in the subnet's
security list / NSG as well — both layers must pass):

```sh
iptables -I INPUT -p udp --dport 51820 -j ACCEPT
```

### Concentrator: tunnel-interface ACCEPT — REQUIRED (OCI default-REJECT caveat)

The concentrator **must ACCEPT traffic arriving on the wanbond tunnel
interface, ordered ahead of any default REJECT rule**:

```sh
iptables -I INPUT -i wanbond0 -j ACCEPT
```

Why this is required: Oracle Cloud (OCI) images ship an INPUT chain ending in

```
-A INPUT -j REJECT --reject-with icmp-host-prohibited
```

which silently applies to the tunnel interface too. ICMP echo is allowed by an
earlier rule, so **ping through the tunnel works while any TCP connection
through the tunnel fails with a confusing `No route to host`** (the REJECT's
ICMP host-prohibited answer). This exact failure was hit during P0 real-host
testing. `iptables -I` (insert at head) places the ACCEPT ahead of the appended
(`-A`) REJECT; verify the ordering with `iptables -S INPUT` — the tunnel ACCEPT
must appear before the `-j REJECT` line. The same applies to any distro or
provider whose default firewall ends INPUT with REJECT/DROP (e.g. firewalld
zones): treat "tunnel ACCEPT ahead of the default reject" as a required
concentrator deployment step.

### Concentrator: persist the rules across reboots — REQUIRED

`iptables -I` mutates only the **runtime** chain. On OCI Ubuntu the
`netfilter-persistent` boot service **restores `/etc/iptables/rules.v4`**, so a
reboot silently discards any runtime-only rule and the tunnel ACCEPT (and the
UDP-port ACCEPT) revert to the default REJECT with no signal until
re-provisioned (defect D7). The rules **must** therefore be written to the boot
rules file. On Debian/Ubuntu:

git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
feat(device): bring wanbond0 administratively UP after TUN creation (I1)

device.Up now sets IFF_UP on the TUN via SIOCSIFFLAGS (golang.org/x/sys/unix,
mirroring the pathsock_linux.go ioctl idiom) right after tun.CreateTUN, closing
the silent-dead-tunnel footgun where writes to a DOWN TUN yield EIO. Addressing
stays operator-owned — Up() only sets the flag, never an address — and the
change lives in the production-only Up() wrapper so the up() seam device unit
tests drive (with an injected channel TUN) is unaffected.

Adds a netns e2e test (test/e2e/link_up_test.go) asserting wanbond0 is UP on
both roles immediately after bring-up with NO external `ip link set up`, and
that the daemon assigns no address; it compiles/vets under -tags e2e but needs
privileged hardware to run (deferred, mirrors the G2 pattern). Documents the
new behavior in docs/install.md's operator-owned addressing section.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"
git log --oneline -1 && git rev-parse HEAD
